items.correct: Yes — plants respire all the time to release energy; they also photosynthesise when there is light.
items.misconception: Plants only photosynthesise and do not respire; respiration is for animals only.
items.random_wrong: Plants only respire at night and only photosynthesise during the day; the processes never overlap.
items.why: Because photosynthesis is taught as the plant equivalent of respiration, students think it replaces respiration in plants entirely.

items.q: Where do plants get most of their biomass from?
items.correct: From carbon dioxide in the air — the carbon in plant tissue comes mostly from CO2 fixed during photosynthesis.
items.misconception: Plants get most of their mass from the soil through their roots.
items.random_wrong: Plants gain most of their mass directly from sunlight, which has weight.
items.why: Roots in soil look like the source of food/mass, but actually most plant dry mass is carbon from atmospheric CO2.

items.q: What is the main role of mitochondria in a cell?
items.correct: To carry out aerobic respiration, producing ATP that cells use as an energy currency.
items.misconception: Mitochondria produce energy by creating it from nothing.
items.random_wrong: Mitochondria store sunlight and release it for photosynthesis.
items.why: The phrase 'powerhouse of the cell' makes students think energy is created by mitochondria, missing that they transfer energy from glucose to ATP.

items.q: Light is X units at 10 cm. At 20 cm it will be:
items.why: Correct answer: X ÷ 4. “X × 2” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is NOT a limiting factor for photosynthesis?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Oxygen concentration. “Light intensity” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Pondweed gives 60 bubbles in 4 min. Mean rate (bubbles/min)?
items.why: The real figure is 15. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
